coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is one of the most common toxic gases, which not only impairs human health but also produces secondary pollutants induced by sunlight via associated photochemical reactions. Herein, we design a high-performance NO2 sensor based on the N-doped carbon dots/SnS2 (N-CDs/SnS2) heterostructure using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The DFT results approve that the decoration of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) onto the SnS2 nanosheet enhances adsorption capacity and electron transportation compared with those of pure SnS2, significantly enhancing the sensing responses. In addition, these in-situ synthesized N-CDs onto SnS2 nanosheet leads to band alignment, thereby significantly improves conductivity and enhances transportation and storage properties of carriers via a synergistic interaction. Furthermore, employment of UV illumination significantly increases active adsorption sites and enhances response and recovery kinetics. As a result, the developed NO2 sensor achieves fast responses of a few seconds and a limit of detection down to sub-ppb-level with the help of UV illumination. It also shows good linearly, operated in both air and oxygen-free environments, at both room or low temperature (238 K) conditions. The developed multi-framework offers an efficient and precision detection of NO2 with ultrafast responses and ultralow concentrations in multiple environments.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots/SnS2 heterostructures for ultra-fast and sub-ppb-level detection of NO2 in multiple environments

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped Carbon Dots/SnS2 Heterostructures for Ultra-fast and Sub-ppb-level Detection of NO2 in Multiple Environments.

Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is one of the most common toxic gases, which not only impairs human health but also produces secondary pollutants induced by sunlight via associated photochemical reactions. Herein, we design a high-performance NO2 sensor based on the N-doped carbon dots/SnS2 (N-CDs/SnS2) heterostructure using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The DFT results approve that the decoration of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) onto the SnS2 nanosheet enhances adsorption capacity and electron transportation compared with those of pure SnS2, significantly enhancing the sensing responses. In addition, these in-situ synthesized N-CDs onto SnS2 nanosheet leads to band alignment, thereby significantly improves conductivity and enhances transportation and storage properties of carriers via a synergistic interaction. Furthermore, employment of UV illumination significantly increases active adsorption sites and enhances response and recovery kinetics. As a result, the developed NO2 sensor achieves fast responses of a few seconds and a limit of detection down to sub-ppb-level with the help of UV illumination. It also shows good linearly, operated in both air and oxygen-free environments, at both room or low temperature (238 K) conditions. The developed multi-framework offers an efficient and precision detection of NO2 with ultrafast responses and ultralow concentrations in multiple environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is one of the most common toxic gases in the current age of urbanization and industrialization. Prolonged exposure to low concentrations of NO2 gas could result in severe illnesses, affecting the respiratory tract, immune system, and neurological disorders [1,2]. In addition, NO2 produces a variety of secondary pollutants, when irradiated with sunlight. For example, photolysis of NO2 is one of the major sources of tropospheric ozone, which is involved in photochemical reactions and forms photochemical aversions [3]. To accurately monitor and effectively avoid these harmful effects of NO2 on human beings’ health and environment, it is urgent to develop technologies that can detect NO2 with high accuracy and low detection limits [4]. Currently, most NO2 sensors are operated in an atmospheric environment either at room temperature or elevated temperatures [5]. In some extreme environments, such as the deep space without oxygen, at the earth poles, or at the high altitudes with low temperatures, there are requirements for the sensor to be operated properly without oxygen at low temperatures, or with influences of many types of other gases [6–8]. These strict and complex environments restrict the successful usages of most currently developed NO2 sensors, as they are mostly relied on the oxygen to involve in sensing reaction and the carriers’ transportation excited by thermal activation [9,10]. Therefore, it is crucially needed, but also super-challenging to design such NO2 sensors which can be operated in such the multiple environments.

For decades, semiconductor-based sensors have been dominant for toxic gas detection (including NO2) due to their simple manufacturing process, high sensitivity and small size [11,12]. The sensitive materials, served as the core component of the sensor, significantly influence the performance of sensors. Among various sensing materials, metal-oxide-semiconductors (MOS) are the most extensively studied and implemented ones, including ZnO, SnO2, TiO2, WO3 and In2O3 [13–15]. Although gas sensors based on these MOS film materials can exhibit high selectivity and stable detection of different gases, they typically need to operate at high temperatures in the range of 150 °C to 400 °C. [16]. What’s more, the functionality of MOS sensors is critically dependent on the formation of adsorbed oxygen ions on their surfaces for sensing capabilities [17]. These limitations significantly restrict their practical applications in the low temperature and oxygen-free environments. Carbon nanomaterials are another crucial category of sensitive materials because of their advantages of large surface areas and outstanding electron transfer efficiency [18]. They lack abundant chemically active sites in their intrinsic structure and therefore cannot specifically adsorb NO2. Surface modification and doping of carbon nanomaterials [19,20] may improve the selectivity and sensitivity toward NO2. Nevertheless, these types of sensors are still suffered from difficulties of sensor’s reversibility and sensing at low temperature environment [21].

Emergence of two-dimensional (2D) layered metal dichalcogenides was reported to overcome the drawbacks of the MOS sensitive materials, and became the prime candidates used for highly sensitive NO2 detection at room temperature (RT) [22,23]. Metal dichalcogenides (e.g., SnS2, MoS2, WS2) are typically comprised of layered S-metal-S structures bonded through weak van der Waals forces, which are characterized by high theoretical specific surface area, high electron mobility, excellent gas molecular adsorption capacity, and easy surface functionalization [24,25]. Among them, SnS2-based sensors have exhibited a good adsorption capability for NO2 and possessed a well electronic energy band structure effective for charge transfers [26]. These advantages make SnS2 more capable of amplifying the advantages of strong oxidizing property of NO2 in the detection process. More importantly, the SnS2 can reduce requirement of surface adsorption of oxygen during sensing [27], which makes it possible for its effective operation in an oxygen-free environment. Recently, many attempts have been made to optimize sensing performance of SnS2 devices via geometrical, electronic, and chemical strategies, achieving a good performance at room temperature [28,29]. Nevertheless, as a candidate material for NO2 sensor, the prepared SnS2 still faces many challenges including its limited surface area, poor conductivity, low carriers mobility, and sluggish response/recovery times [30].

To solve these problems, we have designed a high-performance NO2 sensor based on the N-doped carbon dots/SnS2 (N-CDs/SnS2) heterostructure, which achieved fast responses of a few seconds and a limit of detection down to sub-ppb-level with the help of UV illumination. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were firstly employed to determine the structure of NO2 sensor, and the calculation results prove that the decoration N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) onto the SnS2 nanosheet enhanced adsorption capacity and efficient electron transportation compared with those of pure SnS2, enabling efficient sensing responses. In addition, those in-situ synthesized N-CDs onto SnS2 nanosheet lead to band alignment thereby significantly improve conductivity and enhance transportation and storage properties of carriers via a synergistic interaction. Furthermore, the employment of UV illumination significantly increases active adsorption sites and enhances response and recovery kinetics. The developed NO2 sensor also shows a good linearity in whole concentration range, which can be operated in both air and oxygen-free environments, at room or low temperature conditions. Specifically, the sensor exhibits an ultralow detection limitation (10 ppt), fast response (11 s to 1 ppm), and remarkable selectivity in nitrogen at room temperature. The developed multi-framework offers an efficient and precision detection method for NO2 with ultrafast responses and ultralow concentrations in multiple environments.

Tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O, ≥99 %), thioacetamide (TAA, ≥99 %), L-glutamic acid, o-phenylenediamine, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol absolute and formamide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All the reagents were analytical grade without any purification.

N-CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.11 g of L-glutamic acid and 0.16 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved into 25 ml of DMF. The mixture was subsequently transferred into a 50 ml hydrothermal reactor and heated at 210 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the resultant N-CDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, then precipitated with KOH aqueous solution. Finally, the precipitate was obtained by centrifugation at 12,000 r/min for 30 min, then washed three times with an ethanol/water mixture and dried in vacuum at 40 °C overnight.

The N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructures were synthesized using a simple solvothermal method. As shown in Fig. S1, 0.3506 g of SnCl4·5H2O and 0.1503 g of TAA were dissolved into 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by adding 5 mL of N-CDs/ethanol solution (10 mg/mL). After constant magnetic stirring of the mixture for 30 min, the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 12 h. The final product was washed three times with absolute ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C for 24 h. The different concentrations of N-CDs/ethanol solution of 10, 30, 50, and 100 mg/mL were used for N-CDs/SnS2 preparation and the final products were denoted as N-CDs/SnS2-1, N-CDs/SnS2-2, N-CDs/SnS2-3 and N-CDs/SnS2-4, respectively.

The morphologies, structural information, and elemental distributions of as-prepared materials were characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (VEGA3 LMH), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, USA), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The crystalline phase compositions were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Panalytical X'PERT) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), ranging from 10 to 90 degrees (2θ). Raman spectra were acquired using a Renishaw inVia spectrometer equipped with a 532 nm laser excitation source (incident power of 5 mW). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was conducted using automatic volumetric adsorption equipment (Micromeritics ASAP 2020). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses were conducted using a Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer (NEXUS 370). The energy-band structure of SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2 were validated through a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) using the Thermo Scientific NEXSA instrument.

The sensor was fabricated by drop-casting an ethanol solution containing the sensitive material onto the prepared electrode. The specific procedure involved drop-coating a mixture of N-CDs/SnS2 and ethanol mixture (50 mg/mL) onto an alumina substrate equipped with Ag–Pd interdigitated electrodes, using approximately 20 µL solution. The sensitive material was uniformly distributed across the electrodes (Fig. S2a). The sensor was then annealed at 200 °C for 1 h under a hydrogen/argon atmosphere (5 %/95 %), which was mainly for use in improving the crystallinity of the sensitive material as well as enhancing the conductivity and stability of the sensors. Meanwhile, the annealing process can improve the quality of the contact interface between sensitive materials and the electrodes, thereby enhancing the current for measurement. In addition, by comparing the SEM photographs of the sensitive materials before and after device annealing, it can be seen that there is no change in the morphology (Fig. S2). The entire testing process was photo-activated by irradiating with a UV light source. Place this UV lamp next to the chamber, adjust the UV lamp to 11.5 cm from the N-CDs/SnS2 sensor and shine it vertically on the device with an optical power density of 2.316 mW/cm2 (Fig. S3).The sensing behavior were tested in three environments, e.g., in air at room temperature, in nitrogen at room temperature, and in nitrogen at low temperature.

In air at room temperature: The air conditions used for sensing performance testing refer to the ambient conditions. The temperature of the test is room temperature (about 25°C), the background gas is air, and the humidity is the room humidity (40 %RH-50 %RH). The sensing performance was evaluated using a homemade gas sensing analysis platform equipped with an electrochemical workstation. The air served as the background gas, and a specified volume of the target gas was injected into the test chamber via a syringe to assess the changes in the resistance of the sensitive material under various gas concentrations. The entire testing process was photo-activated by irradiating with a UV light source (375 nm and 2.31 mW/cm2, Fig. S4a). The temperature was the same as that in the room environment.

In nitrogen at room temperature: The high purity nitrogen (99.999 %) was served as the background gas in this scenario at room temperature. Firstly, open the valves of the air inlet and outlet, and continuously pass dry high purity nitrogen into the chamber (1000 sccm/min) for 30 min until the original gas in the chamber is replaced by the nitrogen-filled environment. Subsequently, close the inlet and outlet valves to maintain a nitrogen environment inside the chamber (the chamber is well sealed). Next, 2.5 μL of a 2 ppm standard gas is extracted using a micro-sampler and injected into the chamber (the volume of the chamber is 0.5L, which translates to a NO2 concentration of 10 ppt). At this point, the sensor can detect the NO2 gas in the chamber. During the gas desorption phase, the inlet and outlet valves are opened and nitrogen gas continues to be introduced until all the NO2 is discharged. The operation is then repeated and different target concentrations of NO2 gas are injected and detected by varying the volume of gas extracted by the micro-sampler and the concentration of the standard gas used (Fig. S4b).

In nitrogen at low temperature: The usage of nitrogen gas was the same as above, but the tests were carried out at low temperature from 233 K to 273 K. To realize low temperature, the test chamber and all gas piping were separated from the environment by the heat insulating materials (Fig. S4c). The nitrogen was cooled firstly by liquid nitrogen in advance, and then continuously passed into the highly adiabatic gas chamber. The chamber temperature was regulated by adjusting the cooled nitrogen flow from 230 K to room temperature. For NO2 sensing, a specific NO2 concentration was injected after the valve was closed to maintain a constant chamber pressure. For NO2 desorption, the valve was opened and the cooled nitrogen was continuously introduced to facilitate NO2 desorption. The temperature fluctuations were controlled within 4 °C during the whole tests, with UV light activation maintained throughout. (Fig. S4c).

In the constructed model of N-CDs, the N-CDs are doped with three types of N including pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, and also linked with a multitude of functional groups such as –COOH, –NH2, –OH, and –CHO The Vienna Ab Initio Package (VASP) was employed to perform all the spin-polarized density functional theory (DFT) calculations within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) in the PBE formulation. The projected augmented wave (PAW) potentials were chosen to describe the ionic cores and the valence electrons were taken into account using a plane wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 450 eV. Partial occupancies of the Kohn − Sham orbitals were allowed using the Methfessel-Paxton smearing method and a width of 0.10 eV. The electronic energy was considered as self-consistent when the energy change was smaller than 10−6 eV. A geometry optimization was considered convergent when the residual forces were smaller than 0.05 eV/Å. Finally, the adsorption energies (Eads) were calculated as Eq. (1):
(1)

where Ead/sub, Ead, and Esub are the total energies of the optimized adsorbate/substrate system, the adsorbate in the gas phase, and the clean substrate, respectively.

Although SnS2 has its advantages for NO2 sensing, its large bandgap, poor conductivity, and low carriers’ mobility, hinder its further improved sensing performance. To solve these issues, heterostructures are often employed to tune optical and electrical properties via the band alignment, which is beneficial for the enhancement of carriers’ density, mobility, and separation efficiency. For the usage in gas sensing, increases of specific surface areas, quantity of active sites, and conductivity of the heterostructure should be further considered. For choosing the nanosacled materials to form heterostructure with SnS2, zero-dimensional N-CDs were selected as they offer advantages of easy synthesis, strong optical absorption, distinctive photoinduced electron transfer and storage capabilities. Notably, N-CDs possess a matching energy band with SnS2, which is conducive to promote gas sensing performance. As shown in Fig. 1 a, it illustrates our initial expectations and requirements for the construction of heterogeneous structures, as well as the functions to be achieved. The n-type N-CDs with their higher Fermi energy levels easily transfer electrons to SnS2 until the Fermi energy levels reach an equilibrium. The increase in the Fermi energy level of SnS2 enhances the driving force for charge transfer to NO2, so that the electrons on the surface of SnS2 are more inclined to flow to NO2. DFT calculations were then employed to systematically analyze the adsorption energy and charge transfer characteristics between the target gases including NO2 and pure SnS2 as well as N-CDs/SnS2. For the atomic model for DFT calculations, the CDs contain three types of N (i.e., pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N), which are linked to a variety of functional groups, including –COOH, –NH2, –OH, and –CHO (Fig. 1c). The nearest distances from NO2 molecules to the surface of SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2 are 2.04 Å and 1.93 Å derived from the adsorption modeling (Fig. 1b, c), respectively, which are much shorter than those of H2, NH3, SO2 (Fig. S5). All the distance values are within the range of those for the physical adsorption of gas molecules. Significantly, this type of absorption is readily desorbed from the surface of the N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructure and exhibits less sensitivity to temperature variations [27]. The adsorption energy values between NO2 and SnS2, N-CDs/SnS2 were calculated to be −0.48 eV and −0.76 eV, respectively, which are much larger than those with other gases (i.e., H2, NH3, SO2) and the lattices of SnS2 or N-CDs/SnS2 (Fig. 1d). These findings confirm that NO2 molecules are more easily adsorbed on the N-CDs/SnS2 surface compared with pure SnS2. On the other hand, the N-CDs/SnS2 possesses a higher selectivity toward NO2 compared with other gases. As a result, the large adsorption energy will significantly contribute to the enhanced sensing performance of N-CDs/SnS2 sensors.

Apart from the adsorption energy, the interactions between NO2 and N-CDs/SnS2 were further explored by investigating their charge density differences. Fig. 1e, f show the charge enrichment (blue) and depletion regions (pink) of the combination of NO2 on SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2, respectively. Results show clearly that more significant amounts of charges are depleted from N-CDs/SnS2 and transferred to NO2, achieving a Bader charge value of 0.312 e- Fig. 1f). This value is substantially higher than that of SnS2 (0.197 e-, Fig. 1e). The Bader charge values transferred by N-CDs/SnS2 for the adsorption of H2 (0.066 e-), NH3 (0.085 e-), and SO2 (0.112 e-), are much lower than those for NO2 (Fig. S6). These results are aligned well with their specific adsorption energies. In brief, DFT calculation results confirm that the substantial interactions and extensive charge transfer between the adsorbed NO2 molecules and N-CDs/SnS2 significantly improve the detection capabilities toward NO2.

Densities of states (DOS) within the energy window of −5 to 5 eV (Fig. 1g-i) were calculated using the DFT to further investigate the influences of various gas molecules on the electronic energy band structures of SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2. For the pristine SnS2 (Fig. 1g), the valence band maximum (VBM) is dominated by S3p orbitals, whereas the conduction band minimum (CBM) is primarily composed of S3p and Sn5s states. The pristine SnS2 has an indirect bandgap of 2.17 eV, which is close to the previously reported values [31]. For the N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructure (Fig. 1h), its VBM is mainly consisted of N-CDs and S3p states, whereas its CBM is predominantly governed by orbital of N-CDs. The CBM of N-CDs/SnS2 is observed to shift downward, leading to a reduction in the bandgap to 1.63 eV. The modification of N-CDs with higher high Fermi energy levels helps to regulate the electronic structure of SnS2 and improve its initial conductivity. Therefore, the narrowed bandgap further demonstrates the matching energy band structures between N-CDs and SnS2, which is favorable for the excitation of electrons under the illumination and the realization of highly sensitive detection of NO2 [32].

The obtained DOS values of pristine SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2 with various gas adsorptions are exhibited in Fig. 1i and S7 and S8. For the NH3 and H2 adsorption, the spin-up and spin-down DOS of the nearest-neighbor surfaces of S atoms are symmetric, indicating it is a nonmagnetic system [33]. In contrast, the spin-up and spin-down DOS of the nearest-neighbor surface S atoms are asymmetric for NO2 and SO2 adsorption, indicating that it has a magnetic characteristics [34]. Fig. S8 plots the DOS of H2, NH3 and SO2 adsorbed on the N-CDs/SnS2, which are almost the same as those on the clean surface without gas adsorptions. This indicates that there is a negligible charge transfer between the molecules and the surface of N-CDs/SnS2. Whereas for the adsorption of NO2 molecules, the total DOS and the DOS of S atoms were significantly altered (Figs. 1i and S8). Besides, the conduction band of N-CDs/SnS2 has been shifted to lower energies close to the Fermi energy level, resulting in a decreased bandgap of the system (Fig. 1i). These findings support that there is a strong interaction between NO2 and N-CDs/SnS2, which contributes to the superior sensing responses and good selectivity of N-CDs/SnS2. In overall, the N-CDs possess unique zero-dimensional nanoscale, excellent photogenerated electron transfer capability, and matching energy band structure with SnS2. Additionally, the DFT calculation results demonstrate that N-CDs/SnS2 is more effective for NO2 detection. As a result, the N-CDs/SnS2 heterojunction should be suitable for high-precision detection of NO2 in multiple environments.

After selection of sensitive materials, the N-CDs/SnS2 were synthesized using an in-situ hydrothermal method for SnS2 with N-CDs (see methods). Morphology and microstructures of the N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructures were investigated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2 a and 2b show their distinctive flower-like microstructures with a uniform morphology distribution. The diameter of a single nanoflower is approximately 5 µm (Fig. 2a and b), while the thickness of the petals is ranged from 20 to 40 nm (Fig. 2c). The N-CDs have not been observed on the petals of SnS2 from the SEM images (Fig. 2a–c) due to their nanoscale sizes. Fig. 2d presents a TEM image of N-CDs/SnS2, which shows a microstructure of petal. The enlarged TEM image (Fig. 2e) shows that numerous spherical particles (Fig. 2e yellow circles) are uniformly distributed on the SnS2 nanosheet, demonstrating the successful synthesized N-CDs on the SnS2. The N-CDs’ average size is about 2–7 nm with a uniform distribution (Fig. 2e). Furthermore, a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-CDs/SnS2 (Fig. 2f) reveals lattice fringes of 0.315 nm, 0.297 nm, 0.242 nm and 0.321 nm, which are corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of SnS2 [35], and the (101) and (002) planes of N-CDs [36], respectively. Finally, scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mappings of a single nanoflower show quite uniform distribution of Sn, S, C, N, O elements in the composites (Fig. 2g). All the above results verify the successful synthesis of N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructures, incorporated N-CDs and enriched with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements.

Crystalline structures of the N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructures were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD diffraction peaks of the samples with different concentrations of N-CDs are all similar to those of the standard SnS2 (JCPDF Card 83-1705) without any impurity peaks (Fig. 3 a). Herein, the N-CDs/SnS2-3 represents the addition of 5 mL of N-CDs/ethanol solution (50 mg/mL) in the hydrothermal reaction of N-CDs/SnS2. There is no carbon peak detected in all the samples, probably due to small loading of N-CDs. Raman spectra further demonstrate the successful integration of N-CDs within SnS2, where the peak at 309 cm−1 is attributed to SnS2 and the peaks at 1352 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 are attributed to D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively (Fig. S9) [37].

Specific surface areas of the sensitive material significantly influence the adsorption capacity of the targeted gas, thereby influencing the sensing performance. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm measurements were carried out and the obtained results exhibit a type IV isotherm with an H3 type hysteresis loop (Fig. 3b), indicating the presence of mesopores in both SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 [38,39]. The specific surface areas of the SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 are 26.77 m2/g and 201.80 m2/g calculated via the BET method. The above results demonstrate a significant increase (∼7.54 times) in the specific surface areas of SnS2 decorated with the N-CDs. The increased specific surface areas provide more active sites for adsorption of NO2, which boosts the sensing performance. Furthermore, the pore size distributions of the samples, calculated by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, reveal the average pore sizes of 13.2 nm and 14.8 nm for SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 (Fig. 3b inset), respectively. Obviously, the introduction of N-CDs enlarges the pore space of SnS2, favorable for gas adsorption.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of N-CDs shows a strong emission peak at ∼ 560 nm under the excitation UV wavelength of 375 nm (Fig. S10), which agrees with previous results. The FTIR spectrum reveals that the N-CDs possess a multitude of functional groups (Fig. 3c). Notably, the peaks at 3389, 2942, 1651, and 1084 cm−1 are attributed to those of O–H/N–H, −C-H, −CO, and –C–O, respectively. The peaks at 1639, 1577, and 1453 cm−1 correspond to CN, CC, and C-N stretching vibration modes, respectively, suggesting the formation of a polyaromatic structure within the N-CDs' core [40,41]. The three peaks further show that the N-CDs are doped with nitrogen, which favors the reduction of their work functions.

An X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was utilized to analyze compositions and chemical states of the samples. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S11) reveals the presence of C, N, and O elements in N-CDs. High-resolution spectra reveal the Sn and S elements in SnS2 and the anticipated C, N, O, Sn, and S elements in N-CDs/SnS2. In addition, it can be calculated that the content of N-CDs in the N-CDs/SnS2 samples is about 4.7 % and the N content in the N-CDs samples is about 7.3 %. The high-resolution C1s spectra of both N-CDs and N-CDs/SnS2 can be fitted with four peaks with binding energies of 284.5, 285.7, 286.7, and 288.1 eV (Fig. S12a), corresponding to those of CC/C-C, C-N, C-O, and CO, respectively. The binding energies of C1s in the N-CDs/SnS2 samples exhibit positive shifts compared to those of pure N-CDs, indicating the presence of electronic interactions between N-CDs and SnS2 [42]. The N1s spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/SnS2 show three peaks at 398.4, 399.4, and 400.9 eV, which are attributed to those of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. The estimated percentages of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N in the N-CDs samples are 52 %, 28 %, and 20 %, respectively, while they are 47 %, 36 %, and 17 % for the N-CDs/SnS2 samples, respectively (Fig. 3d). These results clearly demonstrate that N-CDs contain multiple types of N, which endows the N-CDs to have an enhanced electron transfer capability, thereby improving the sensing properties of N-CDs/SnS2. Meanwhile, the O1s spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/SnS2 display two peaks at 531.1 and 532.6 eV, associated with CO and C-O bands, respectively (Fig. S12b) [43,44]. Consistent with the C1s spectrum, the CO and C-O bands in the O1s spectrum of N-CDs/SnS2 also exhibit positive shifts of binding energies compared with those of the pure SnS2.

Furthermore, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was employed to obtain optical properties and band gaps of N-CDs, SnS2, and N-CDs/SnS2-3 (Fig. 3e and Fig. S13). The N-CDs demonstrate the light-absorbing capabilities across the entire UV–Visible spectrum and the pronounce optical absorption in the UV region (Fig. S13a). The N-CDs/SnS2-3 sample shows an enhanced absorption in the visible region compared to those of SnS2 (Fig. 3e), which promotes the generation of a significant number of photogenerated carriers under the visible light excitation. In addition, the optical bandgaps of SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 were calculated to be 2.13 eV and 1.98 eV via the Kubelka-Munk equation(Fig. 3f) [45], respectively. The N-CDs/SnS2-3 composites show the reduced bandgaps and enhanced conductivities, compared to those of the pristine SnS2 [46,47], The plots of Mott-Schottky relationships of the SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 samples (Fig. 3g) show positive slopes, demonstrating their n-type semiconductor nature. Additionally, the conduction band potentials of N-CDs, SnS2, and N-CDs/SnS2-3 with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were calculated based on the Mott-Schottky plots, which are −0.29 V, −0.21 V and −0.27 V (Figs. 3g and S14), respectively (i.e., the potential vs NHE equals Potential vs Ag/AgCl minus 0.2).

To further explore the energy band structures of these samples, UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was employed to obtain the work functions (φ) of SnS2 and N-CDs (Fig. 3h). The high binding energy region of the UPS spectra was utilized to determine the secondary electron cut-off energy (Ecut-off), and then the work functions were obtained (work function φ = 21.22 eV-Ecut-off, where 21.22 eV is the energy of the UV-source). The obtained results indicate that the work functions of SnS2 and N-CDs are 5.02 eV and 4.43 eV (Fig. 3h), respectively, which are similar to those values reported in the literature for SnS2 [48] and N-doped carbon dots [49,50]. It is evident that the energy band structures of N-CDs and SnS2 are consistent with our designs (Fig. 3i). The work function of N-CDs is smaller than that of SnS2, the electrons can spontaneously flow from N-CDs to SnS2 until the Fermi energy levels reach an equilibrium. The decorated N-CDs enhance the electrons’ concentrations and also the conductivity of SnS2, which make it easier for the absorbed NO2 to capture electrons from SnS2. In addition, the modification of N-CDs reduces the band gap of N-CDs/SnS2 and enhances the absorption and utilisation of UV light. Under the excitation of UV light, it is easier to separate a large number of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in N-CDs/SnS2, further increasing the electron concentration of N-CDs/SnS2-3. This enables fast charge transfer with a large number of NO2 molecules, thus improving the sensing performance. In brief, we demonstrate that N-CDs/SnS2-3 exhibits superior responses and sensitivity for the detection of NO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance in air at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: We have evaluated the developed N-CDs/SnS2 NO2 sensor at the different environments and under the UV excitation, by monitoring the resistance changes upon NO2 gas exposure. All the responses of gas sensors based on SnS2, N-CDs/SnS2-1, N-CDs/SnS2-2, N-CDs/SnS2-3, and N-CDs/SnS2-4 samples to 0.5 ppm NO2 under UV illumination were recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. These N-CDs/SnS2 sensors exhibit superior gas-sensitizing properties compared to that of pristine SnS2. Significantly, the N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensor demonstrates the largest resistance changes (Fig. 4a, the purple curve). Whereas that of N-CDs/SnS2-4 with the highest content of N-CDs does not show the best sensing performance, probably due to the potential clogging of SnS2 pores by adding the excessive N-CDs and reduction in their catalytic activity sites [46]. In the following tests, the sensing performance was evaluated using the N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensors, except specifically mentioned.

The responses of SnS2 and N-CDs/SnS2-3 sensors were then compared by illuminating them with lights with different wavelengths but the same optical density (2.31 mW/cm2) and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S15. The largest response was achieved from the UV light with 375 nm wavelength which is related to the optimal light absorption of N-CDs/SnS2-3 (Fig. 3e). It is noteworthy that the response times are more or less similar under different incident light wavelengths, but a longer recovery time was found for the case with the larger wavelengths. Fig. 4b shows the dynamic responses of the N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensor to NO2 measured in air at room temperature (the base resistance value is 0.83 MΩ under UV excitation). The electrical resistance is increased with the increase of NO2 concentration, and does not reach a saturation value over a large concentration range from 0.05 ppm to 10 ppm (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c demonstrates a quite good linear relationship between the sensor responses and the NO2 concentrations. Significantly, the sensor resistance can return to its initial resistance without showing apparent baseline drift, which is quite important for the actual usages. Fig. 4d plots the sensor responses to an ultralow NO2 concentration of 10 ppb, which still shows a response value of 1.2 % and maintains a good signal-to-noise ratio. It highlights the sensor's ultra-low detection limitation, which is much better than those of most previously reported sensors as listed in Supplementary Table 1. The response time was measured to be as fast as 9 s to 1 ppm NO2 (Fig. 4e), which is an extraordinary record for the NO2 sensing in air at room temperature. The recovery time was measured to be about 132 s, which is comparable with the previous results from literature (Table S1). Both these parameters are decreased significantly under the UV illumination, if compared with those obtained in visible light or a dark condition (Fig. S15). This is mainly because the adsorption and desorption rates of NO2 molecules on the N-CDs/SnS2-3 surface are significantly enhanced by using the UV irradiation. The fast response will be benefit for its actual usage in future.

Fig. 4f plots the sensor’s continuous responses to 0.5 ppm NO2, which demonstrates its considerable repeatability and stability within an acceptable error tolerance range. Furthermore, the sensor was subjected to a response-recovery cycles for detection of NO2 with varied.

concentrations of NO2 (i.e., 10, 100, 300, 600, 800 and 1000 ppb) (Fig. 4g inset), and the results show the sensor’s good hysteresis characteristics. The sensor's response values are remained essentially unchanged under both increasing and decreasing concentration conditions, indicating its minimal detection hysteresis and high detection accuracy (Fig. 4g). This low hysteresis is an essential for practical application. In addition, we have tested the long-term stability of the sensing material when it was exposed to NO2 at a concentration of 0.2 ppm for nearly 30 days (under the ambient environment storage). Fig. 4h demonstrates that there is only a small variation in the response of the sensor during the long-term test. It indicates a considerable stability even under the repeated UV illumination. Meanwhile, the sensor remained stable during a 5-cycle response test on the 28th day (inset in Fig. 4h).

The sensor selectivity was assessed by evaluating its responses to various commonly used gases including NO2, SO2, CO2, CO, NH3, H2, with their concentrations of 1 ppm and 5 ppm (Fig. 4i). The sensor exhibits the largest response to NO2 compared to those of other gases (Fig. 4i), which demonstrates its good selectivity of the sensor to NO2. The results correspond to the adsorption energy (−0.76 eV) and Bader charge values (0.312 e-) of N-CDs/SnS2 for NO2 in the DFT calculation. This is because SnS2 has a strong electron affinity for paramagnetic NO2 molecules than other gas molecules. Besides, the modification of N-CDs with multiple functional groups provides SnS2 with larger specific surface area, more active sites and continuous supply of electronics, which further promotes the adsorption and sensing of NO2 molecules on the sensor layer material. This further makes N-CDs/SnS2 has better adsorption and selectivity for NO2 than other gases[51]. Furthermore, the sensor responses were recorded both in NO2 and mixture gases (including NO2, SO2, CO2, CO, NH3, H2) with the certain concentrations (i.e., 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 ppm) (Fig. S16). The responses are varied only slightly, which demonstrates its excellent selectivity and detection independence of the sensor for NO2 gas. In addition, the sensing response values under different humidity levels against 1 ppm NO2 were measured. Fig. S17 exhibits the sensor responses as humidity change from 10 % RH to 90 %RH, in which it reduces from 1.29 to 0.87. The possible reason for this phenomenon is that when the relative humidity increases, the water molecules will cover the sensing film, preventing the contact between NO2 molecules and N-CDs/SnS2-3, resulting in a low response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance in nitrogen at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies showed that the SnS2-based sensor was relied on the physical adsorption model, which did not need the surface adsorption of oxygen to performance the sensing process.[27] Herein, the sensing performance of the N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensor was investigated in an oxygen-free environment (pure nitrogen) at room temperature (Fig. 5 ). In the pure nitrogen, there are more active sites exposed on the N-CDs/SnS2-3 surface for NO2 adsorption, whereas some of which were occupied by oxygen molecules in air before. Consequently, the sensor in nitrogen could achieve an enhanced dynamic response and a much lower LOD if compared to those in air at room temperature. Fig. 5a plots the sensor’s continuous responses to NO2 as its concentration increased from 0.01 ppb to 2000 ppb in nitrogen (the base resistance value is 0.22 MΩ under UV excitation), which exhibits enhanced responses compared with those in air (Fig. 4b). Fig. 5b plots the sensor’s responses as a function of NO2 concentrations. The curves can be fitted well by a linear equation after 20 ppb, with a slope value of 5.57 in nitrogen at room temperature (Fig. 5b). This value is ∼ 5 times higher than that in air at room temperature (Fig. 4c). It is noteworthy to address that the sensor still shows a 15.2 % response at the ultralow concentration of 0.01 ppb NO2 (Fig. 5c). The LOD values could be further decreased if a precisely lower NO2 concentration can be prepared experimentally. Impressively, the responses have been improved by ∼ 13 times (from 1.2 % to 15.2 %), even after the NO2 was diluted by 10,000 times (from 10 ppb to 0.01 ppb) (Fig. 4d and Fig. 5c).

Moreover, the sensor demonstrated exceptional response and recovery kinetics under the UV excitation in an oxygen-free environment, with its response and recovery times of 11 s and 139 s, when the sensor was exposed to 1 ppm NO2 (Fig. 5d), respectively. The obtained parameters were similar to those recorded in air. Fig. 5e shows the sensor’s responses to successive exposure to 0.2, 20, and 200 ppb of NO2, and consistent responses and recovery cycles with minimal variations have been achieved, indicating the sensor's good repeatability. The hysteresis characteristic curve in Fig. 5f shows the sensor’s responses to 0.02, 0.2, 20, 80, 140 and 200 ppb of NO2. The sensor showed good linearity in the NO2 concentration range of 20 ppb-2000 ppb. The curve illustrates that the variance did not surpass 5.0 % as the NO2 concentration was increased from 0.02 to 200 ppb and then returned back. The sensor exhibits very small hysteresis, which is benefit for practical application. The changes in the response of the sensor to 0.1 ppm NO2 on the first day and after 40 days were further obtained (Fig. 5g). The results show an excellent long-.

term stability in this operating environment. The sensor’s selectivity was evaluated via exposing it to various gases with different concentrations from 2 ppm to 10 ppm (Fig. 5h). Impressively, the response values to CO2, CO, NH3, and H2 were almost zero, whereas they were 0.11 and 1.27 for SO2, which was one order smaller than those in NO2. Therefore, the sensor shows quite a good selectivity in mixed gases, which is benefit for practical applications. Furthermore, the sensor exhibits consistent response values for identical concentrations of NO2 and the aforementioned gas mixtures (Fig. 5i) with various concentrations. In addition, in order to reveal the differences between devices, six N-CDs/SnS2-3 sensors fabricated in the same lot are provided in Fig. S18. It can be seen that the initial resistance differences and the gas-sensitive response differences are within acceptable limits in air/ nitrogen at room temperature, which proves that the devices have good consistency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance in nitrogen at low temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, there were few studies about the gas sensor’s performance at lower temperatures (or sub-room temperatures) based on the 2D materials or their heterostructures. As it is well known, most of semiconductor materials show fast reduced carrier concentrations and mobilities at low temperatures, leading to significant degradation of device’s performance. We investigated the sensing performance of N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensor in nitrogen at a low temperature. Fig. 6 a depicts continuous responses to 0.5 ppm NO2 in nitrogen when the temperature is decreased from 273 K to 233 K. The responses to 0.5 ppm NO2 are found to diminish as temperature is decreased. Moreover, the recovery process is very slow compared to tests at room temperature.

The sensor’s performance was further tested with 0.1–10 ppm NO2 in a pure nitrogen environment at 238 K (the base resistance value is 2.65 MΩ under UV excitation) (Fig. 6b). The sensor’s responses are changed linearly with the increase of NO2 concentration (Fig. 6c). The change rate was fitted with a value of 0.497 at 238 K, which is much smaller than that in air (Fig. 4c) and only one-tenth of that in nitrogen (Fig. 5b) at room temperature. Fig. 6d depicts the sensor’s responses to 0.1 ppm NO2, resulting in 3.1 % at low temperature. To achieve the similar response level, the NO2 concentration can be adjusted below 0.01 ppb in nitrogen or below 10 ppb in air and at room temperature. Although there is degraded performance in detection sensitivity, LOD, and response at low temperature (Fig. 6b-d), those obtained parameters are still competitive with those previously reported results (Table S1). Additionally, the sensor achieved response/recovery times of 29 s/204 s for 1 ppm NO2 (Fig. 6e), which are a little longer than those obtained at room temperature (Fig. 4e, Fig. 5d), due to the reduction of carriers’.

mobilities at lower temperatures. Besides, the repeatability tests (Fig. 6f) of the sensor were performed by cyclically exposed it to the same NO2concentrations of 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 ppm, and the results showed stable responses for each concentration. The hysteresis tests (Fig. 6g) over a concentration range between 0.5 to 5.0 ppm also show a stable sensing performance. In addition, the long-term stability test in Fig. 6h indicates that the sensor still exhibits essentially unchanged response to 0.5 ppm NO2 after 32 days and remained very stable during a 5-cycle response test on the 32th day. It should be addressed that the sensor demonstrated an enhanced selectivity at low temperatures in nitrogen (Fig. 6i), surpassing its room temperature counterparts (Fig. 4i, Fig. 5h). Furthermore, the sensor exhibited consistent responses for the identical concentrations of NO2 mixed with specified gas mixtures (Fig. S19) in various concentrations.

All the results above showed the competitive performance of the sensor at 238 K in oxygen-free environment, which highlights the sensor's advantages and potential for the NO2 detection at low temperatures. However, the sensing’s performance shows slightly degraded compared with those at room temperature, which is attributed to the slow diffusion of gas, sluggish responses and recovery kinetics, reduction in carrier concentrations and their mobilities at low temperatures. Table S1 also shows gas sensing performance of the sensor at a low temperature and those of previously published MDCs-based sensors for NO2 at room temperature. Results show that the sensor in this work exhibits excellent sensor capability toward NO2 under multiple environments, including low detection limit, high response and considerable response/recovery characteristics.

In this designed sensing system, N-CDs/SnS2 is served as the sensitive material for NO2 detection with the assistance of UV light irradiation. For the resistive types of gas sensors, the essential part is the target gas molecules’ adsorption onto the sensitive material, which causes charge transfer between them [52]. As an n-type semiconductor, N-CDs/SnS2′s electrical resistance is primarily dependent on the electron concentrations and mobilities. The physically adsorbed NO2 gas molecules act as electron acceptors, which will capture electrons from N-CDs/SnS2 as shown in the Eq. (2), thereby reducing its conductivity [53]. In air, oxygen molecules will be adsorbed on the surface of N-CDs/SnS2 in advance and occupy some of the active sites, trapping electrons from the conduction band according to Eq. (3). As shown in Fig. S20, the adsorption of oxygen on the surface of N-CDs/SnS2 was modeled and the differential charge density was calculated by DFT. It can be seen that the N-CDs/SnS2 surface is depleted of electrons, while oxygen acts as an electron acceptor surface accumulating a large number of electrons. And the Bader charge transfer value between the two is calculated to be 0.106 e-. The interaction (Eq. (3)) will result in the creation of an electron depletion layer on the N-CDs/SnS2 surface, thereby increasing its electrical resistance. This demonstrates a competitive relationship between O2 and NO2 in the sensing process.
(2)
NO2(gas)+ e- → NO2 − (abs)
(3)
O2 (gas) + e- → O2 − (abs)

Upon exposure to NO2, the gas molecules will first adsorb onto SnS2 and grab electrons from it (the green arrow in Fig. 7 ), increasing the resistance of SnS2. Next, the N-CDs with their lower work functions transfers electrons to SnS2 (the red arrow in Fig. 7), which further assists the adsorption and sensing of NO2 molecules on the sensing layer materials. Both N-CDs and SnS2 have excellent absorption and transfer abilities for photogenerated electrons in the UV region. In addition, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs on N-CDs are effectively separated at the N-CDs/SnS2 surface, which further increases the electron concentration in SnS2 and makes it easier to transfer the electrons to NO2 for excellent sensing. In air at room temperature (Fig. 7a), both O2 and NO2 are adsorbed on the surface of SnS2 and electrons are captured from itself (the pink and green arrows shown in Fig. 7a), establishing a competitive dynamic relationship. The oxygen could impede the electron transfer between NO2 and SnS2, although it has a lower electron affinity compared to that of NO2.The NO2 will compete with O2 for active sites on the N-CDs/SnS2 surfaces and captures more electrons from its conduction band to form NO2− (Eq. (1)) since it has a significantly higher electron affinity compared with that of oxygen [54]. As a result, the depletion layer becomes wider, thereby the electrical resistance is further increased.

In a nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature, there is no competition from O2 for adsorption on active sites and capturing of the electrons from the N-CDs/SnS2. All the active sites are occupied by NO2 molecules which forms a wide depletion layer, leading to a significant increase in electrical resistance (Fig. 7b). This new mechanism underpins the superior gas-sensitive performance of the N-CDs/SnS2 sensor in the nitrogen environment at room temperature compared with its counterpart in air. In nitrogen at low temperature (Fig. 7c, 238 K), the diffusion, adsorption and desorption rates of NO2 underwent a significant reduction, whereas the carrier concentration and mobility in the N-CDs/SnS2 are decreased significantly. As a result, both the quantity of NO2 molecules adsorbed on the N-CDs/SnS2 surface and the associated electron transfer are decreased.
In brief, the superior sensing performance of N-CDs/SnS2-3 based sensor at three types of environments (Fig. 7) could be attributed to the following three reasons:

Firstly, the large specific surface area of the N-CDs/SnS2 supplies more active sites for NO2 adsorption. It has been increased from 26.77 m2/g to 201.80 m2/g after N-CDs’ decoration onto SnS2 nanoflower structure. These values are much higher than those of previously reported SnS2-based sensitive materials [55–57]. For the N-CDs/SnS2-3 samples, the quantity of N-CDs is sufficient and well dispersed, which increases the specific surface areas, but not blocks the pores simultaneously.

Secondly, the 0D-2D heterojunction formed by N-CDs/SnS2-3 significantly improves conductivity and charge transfer efficiency during the sensing process. To better understand this feature, the energy band diagrams of N-CDs and SnS2 are redrawn according to the experimental data as shown in Fig. 3i. Both the N-CDs and SnS2 exhibit n-type behavior, and the work function of N-CDs is smaller than that of SnS2. The electrons transferred from N-CDs to SnS2 enhance the driving force for sensing between SnS2 and NO2, and the electrons on the surface of SnS2 are more inclined to flow towards NO2 [53]. Moreover, the absorbed NO2 molecules can trap electrons from the heterojunction during the sensing process, which breaks the charge balance within the N-CDs/SnS2-3 heterostructure. The built-in electric field prompts a rapid replenishment of electrons from the N-CDs to SnS2, enhancing the energy bands' bending. However, an excessive coverage of N-CDs results in their aggregation, which reduces the number of effective hetero-interfaces and reduces the sensor's sensitivity [58].

Thirdly, activation of UV light significantly improves the sensitivity and response/recovery kinetics of the sensor. The optical bandgap of the N-CDs/SnS2-3 is 1.98 eV, which is smaller than that of pure SnS2 (2.13 eV) (Fig. 3f). It exhibits an enhanced absorption with wavelengths from 200 to 500 nm (Fig. 3e). As a result, the N-CDs/SnS2-3 heterostructure can be more efficiently generated and separate a quantity of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (h(hv) +, e(hv) -) under the UV illumination, which contributes to the improved performance of the sensor in detecting NO2 [59]. The photogenerated electrons can boost the electron concentrations of N-CDs/SnS2-3, which enables its reaction with numerous NO2 molecules (Eq. (4)), leading to an enhanced response. On the contrary, the photogenerated holes can remove NO2− from the surface of N-CDs/SnS2-3 during the recovery phase (Eq. (5)), which facilitates the desorption of NO2 from the sensitive material and recovers many active sites [60,61]. Besides, the built-in electric field at the N-CDs/SnS2-3 heterojunctions effectively prevents the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby extending the lifetime of these carriers. The highly reactive photogenerated electrons accelerate the adsorption–desorption rate, which ultimately provide the sensor with a faster response and recovery under UV illumination. Consequently, the N-CDs/SnS2-3 exhibits exceptional detection sensitivity and swift response recovery kinetics, even at low temperatures [62].
(4) NO2(gas) + e(hν) − → NO2 − (ads)
(5) NO2 − (ads) + h(hν) + → NO2(gas)

In summary, we have designed and developed the N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructure based gas sensor for fast detection NO2 at the sub-ppb level operated in multiple environments. The N-CDs/SnS2 heterostructure have superior adsorption capacity and electron transfer efficiency to NO2 according to theoretical calculations. The sensor demonstrates exceptional performances characterized by an ultralow detection threshold (10 ppt), rapid response kinetics (11 s), and superior selectivity across all tested conditions. The large specific surface area and 0D-2D heterojunction of N-CDs/SnS2 as well as the UV light activation, contribute to the enhanced NO2 sensing performance. This work underscores the importance of heterostructure engineering and light activation in the design of sensitive and selective gas sensors, opening new avenues for the detection of NO2 at low concentrations in more specialized scenarios.

Ping Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xinxin He: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xuyang An: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xingyu Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Shuai Liang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
